data.title: Tootsie Roll among names set to join Russell 3000 Index, Growgeneration, Veru set to leave
data.description:
FTSE Russell has released a preliminary list of companies set to join or leave the Russell 3000 Index as part of its 2025 annual reconstitution.

The consumer staple companies that are slated to join the index include: Tootsie Roll Industries (NYSE:TR), Lifevantage (NASDAQ:LFVN), Zevia (NYSE:ZVIA), Fitlife Brands (NASDAQ:FTLF), and Wellgistics Health (NASDAQ:WGRX)

The consumer staple names that are leaving the index include Growgeneration (NASDAQ:GRWG), Veru (NASDAQ:VERU), and Cibus (NASDAQ:CBUS).

The final inclusion is expected to take effect after the U.S. market opens on June 30, as part of the Russell indexes’ annual reconstitution, effective after market close on June 27, 2025.

data.title: Beyond Meat, Plby among consumer staple names set to join Russell Microcap Index, Veru, Vitals Farms to leave
data.description:
FTSE Russell released a preliminary list of companies set to join or leave the Russell Microcap Index as part of its 2025 annual reconstitution.

The consumer staple companies set to join the Russell Microcap Index are Beyond Meat (NASDAQ:BYND), Plby Group (NASDAQ:PLBY), Laird Superfood (NYSE:LSF), Zevia (NYSE:ZVIA), and Barfresh Food Group (NASDAQ:BRFH), among others.

The consumer staple companies set to leave the Russell Microcap Index are Veru (NASDAQ:VERU), Vital Farms (NASDAQ:VITL), The Vita Coco Company (NASDAQ:COCO), Natural Alternatives (NASDAQ:NAII), and Synergy Chc (NASDAQ:SNYR).

These changes will be effective after U.S. equity markets close on June 27.

data.title: Japan sees strongest retail sales in 3 months; factory output contraction eases; Tokyo core inflation tops forecasts
data.description:
Retail sales in Japan grew by 3.3% year-on-year in April 2025, up from a 3.1% gain in the previous month and surpassing market expectations of a 3.1% rise, marking the 37th consecutive month of retail sales growth, with rising wages continuing to support consumption.

On a monthly basis, retail sales increased by 0.5% in April, rebounding from a 1.2% fall in March.

Japan’s industrial production fell by 0.9% month-over-month in April 2025, reversing a 0.2% gain in March but performing better than market expectations of a 1.1% drop, flash data showed.

On a yearly basis, industrial output rose by 0.7% in April, easing from a 1.0% increase in March but marking the third consecutive month of growth.

Domestically, Tokyo’s core inflation came in stronger than expected, rose 3.6% year-on-year in May 2025, accelerating from a 3.4% increase in April and above market expectations of 3.5%, reinforcing expectations of further Bank of Japan rate hikes. 

The Nikkei 225 Index dropped 1.5% to below 38,000 while the broader Topix Index tumbled 0.9% to 2,785 on Friday, reversing gains from the previous session. The Japanese yen appreciated past 144 per dollar on Friday, extending gains from the previous session, after Tokyo’s core inflation came in stronger than expected. 

More on Japan

  • Japan's manufacturing PMI revised upward to 48.7 in April

  • Japan to spend ¥388 billion on relief measures for U.S. tariff impact

  • Japan producer prices rise more than expected to 4.2% in March

  • Japan’s unemployment rate unexpectedly drops to 2.4% in February

  • Japan's Feb industrial production exceeds expectations, retail sales fall short of forecasts

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM International Trade in Goods (Advance) 
 
This monthly report offers advance import and export data on the goods components of the monthly trade report. Goods make up roughly two-thirds of the nation's exports and roughly three-quarters of imports.
 
After reaching an amazing $162.0 billion deficit in March as importers stocked up ahead of import tariffs, forecasters see the trade gap at a still-wide $143.0 billion in April.
 
8:30 AM Personal Income and Outlays 
 
Personal income represents the income that households receive from all sources including wages and salaries, fringe benefits such as employer contributions to private pension plans, proprietors' income, income from rent, dividends and interest and transfer payments such as Social Security and unemployment compensation. 
 
Personal income seen up 0.3 percent, spending up 0.2 percent on the month. PCE prices seen rising by 0.1 percent on the month and 0.1 percent for the core.
 
8:30 AM Retail Inventories (Advance) 
 
Retail inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by retailers. The advance report is released late in the month for the following month and is part of the Monthly Advance Economic Indicators report (which also includes data on wholesale inventories and international trade in goods). 
 
8:30 AM Wholesale Inventories (Advance) 
 
Wholesale trade measures the dollar value of sales made and inventories held by merchant wholesalers. It is a component of business sales and inventories.
Forecasters see no revision from up 0.5 percent in the flash.
 
9:45 AM Chicago PMI 
 
The Institute For Supply Management - Chicago compiles a survey and a composite diffusion index of business conditions in the Chicago area. 
 
The call looks for 45.0 in May versus 44.6 in April as a nasty contraction continues in Chicagoland.
 
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment 
 
The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.
 
Forecasters see the index at 52.0 in the final May report, versus a low-low 50.8 in the preliminary report for May.
 
12:20 PM Fed's Bostic Speech 
 
Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic gives welcome remarks before the Council for Economic Education's 2025 National Economics Challenge.
 
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. Used for drilling wellbores for wells that may eventually produce oil or gas, active rigs are essential for the exploration and development of oil and gas fields. 
 
4:45 PM Fed's Daly Speech 
 
San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in panel before virtual Reagan National Economic Forum.
 
7:30 PM Fed's Goolsbee Speech 
 
Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will participate in a moderated Q&A before"The Interview Show with Mark Bazer"

data.title: PagerDuty outlines $493M–$499M FY26 revenue target as enterprise sales model shifts and AI expansion advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PagerDuty (PD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jennifer Tejada, Chief Executive Officer, reported first quarter revenue of $120 million, reflecting 8% growth and at the top of guidance, and emphasized a "relentless focus on disciplined cost and investment management" that drove a non-GAAP operating margin of 20%, which exceeded the company’s target by 500 basis points. She stated, "Given the substantial process we have made on optimizing our bottom line performance, we have a clear path to GAAP profitability."
  • Annual recurring revenue increased to $496 million, with 7% growth year-over-year. Dollar-based net retention was 104%, cited as impacted by "higher-than-expected customer downgrades in our enterprise segment and elevated churn in our commercial business." Tejada explained the company is committed to "more consistent sales and marketing execution and efficiency" and has taken "immediate and comprehensive action to improve overall top line results."
  • PagerDuty achieved its largest net new paid customer increase in eight quarters, attributed to a new commercial digital acquisition strategy. The company is "evolving our coverage model" toward "more strategic cross-company relationships."
  • Tejada highlighted a strengthened partnership with AWS and announced FedRAMP Low authorization, enabling expansion in the public sector, with active pursuit of FedRAMP Moderate authorization.
  • The company is "on track to launch three additional AI agents this quarter," with expanded AI integration and new products such as AI Scribe agent and enhanced generative AI features.
  • Board expansion included welcoming Don Carty, and the search for a new Chief Revenue Officer was noted as progressing well.
  • Howard Wilson, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "More than 60% of our enterprise reps will have been with PagerDuty for at least a year by the end of the second quarter, reflecting our strategic investment in experienced enterprise talent focused on higher value, more profitable customer relationships."

Outlook

  • For Q2 fiscal 2026, revenue is expected between $122.5 million and $124.5 million, with net income per diluted share in the range of $0.19 to $0.20 and an implied operating margin of 17%.
  • Full fiscal year 2026 revenue is now projected at $493 million to $499 million, representing a growth rate of 5% to 7%. This is a reduction from the previous guidance of $500 million to $507 million.
  • Full-year net income per diluted share is expected to be between $0.95 and $1, up from the prior guide of $0.90 to $0.95, with an implied operating margin of 20% to 21% (previously 19% to 20%).
  • Wilson described the guidance as "prudent" and noted it accounts for "organizational transitions and some of the go-to-market execution challenges we had in Q1."

Financial Results

  • Q1 revenue was $120 million, with international revenue increasing 11% annually and contributing 28% of total revenue.
  • Annual recurring revenue ended at $496 million.
  • Dollar-based net retention was 104% and is expected to remain between 103% and 105% for fiscal 2026.
  • Customers spending over $100,000 in annual recurring revenue totaled 848, up 5% year-over-year. Total paid customers reached 15,247, with 127 net new customers in Q1.
  • Gross margin was 86%. Non-GAAP operating income was $24 million, or 20% of revenue. Cash from operations for the quarter was $31 million. The company ended the quarter with $597 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments.
  • Trailing 12-month billings were $492 million, up 7%. Total remaining performance obligations stood at approximately $430 million.

Q&A

  • Robert Oliver, Baird, asked about enterprise pressure and the impact of rapid innovation in generative AI on multiyear contracts. CEO Tejada responded, "It's primarily execution...combination of moving some reps out of the business, bringing new reps into the business, reassigning territories...just led to some of those coverage gaps."
  • Oliver followed up on guidance prudence. CFO Wilson replied, "I would describe the guidance that we provided as being prudent. The primary input...was based on the organizational transitions and some of the go-to-market execution challenges we had in Q1."
  • Jamie, Morgan Stanley, inquired about adoption trends for new modules. Tejada said, "It's still early days...we have customers that are very willing to experiment and other customers have to go through a significant process to get permission to experiment" and noted positive feedback for generative AI products.
  • Miller Jump, Truist, requested more color on enterprise churn. Tejada explained churn in SMB and downgrades in enterprise, attributing some to company mergers and cautious behavior from customers due to macro uncertainty. Wilson added, "We have introduced a number of post-sales changes...which is really changing the way in which we think about renewal management in a far more proactive way."
  • Matt Bullock, BofA, discussed billings indicators. Wilson clarified that trailing 12 months billings align more closely with annual recurring revenue than quarterly billings.
  • Andrew Sherman, TD Cowen, asked about the AI research company win and expansion opportunities. Tejada described the deal as "a leading AI research and development company selected PagerDuty for a 6-figure multiyear commitment" and highlighted opportunities in new AI operation use cases.
  • Daniel Hibshman, Craig-Hallum, questioned the upward profitability guide revision. Wilson responded, "We have a track record of improving operating margins...the investments that we've made to date still provide us with capacity to be able to exceed our growth goals but also ensure that we're continuing to invest in innovation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly cautious, pressing on enterprise churn, sales execution, and prudence in guidance, with detailed follow-ups on guidance rationale and adoption trends.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational discipline and strategic actions, but displayed more defensive and explanatory responses in Q&A, particularly regarding churn and sales coverage gaps. Tejada asserted, "It's primarily execution," and Wilson used "prudent" to describe guidance, reflecting measured caution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions shifted more toward details of churn and tangible sales execution, while management’s tone was somewhat more defensive but remained confident about long-term prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The latest quarter saw a reduction in full-year revenue guidance to $493 million–$499 million from $500 million–$507 million, signaling a more cautious outlook amid sales execution challenges.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin guidance improved to 20%–21% from 19%–20%, and EPS guidance increased.
  • Management highlighted the transition to a more experienced enterprise sales team and immediate actions on sales coverage and post-sales engagement, compared to broader leadership changes and strategic shifts mentioned last quarter.
  • Analyst focus shifted from market opportunity and large deal wins to detailed questions about enterprise churn, guidance prudence, and sales ramping.
  • Management’s confidence in long-term growth and profitability remained, but with greater emphasis on acknowledging internal execution issues impacting near-term performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "higher-than-expected customer downgrades in our enterprise segment and elevated churn in our commercial business."
  • Execution gaps due to "moving some reps out of the business, bringing new reps into the business, reassigning territories" were flagged as causes for sales coverage issues.
  • Macro uncertainty and customer caution were noted as factors in both churn and deal downgrades.
  • Management outlined mitigation strategies including enhanced post-sale enterprise engagement, expansion of the Chief Customer Officer’s role, and ongoing transition to a more experienced sales force.

Final Takeaway

PagerDuty’s first quarter results reflected solid operating margin expansion and ongoing product innovation, but revenue guidance for the full year was lowered as the company addressed sales execution challenges and customer churn. Management expressed confidence that investments in enterprise sales talent, expanded AI offerings, and strengthened strategic partnerships will support improved results and long-term growth, while maintaining a disciplined approach to profitability and capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elastic outlines 12% revenue growth target for FY 2026 amid strong GenAI momentum and strategic partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Elastic N.V. (ESTC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ashutosh Kulkarni highlighted a strong quarter for Elastic, stating, "We exceeded guidance across all revenue and profitability metrics, demonstrating our platform strength and the extraordinary efforts of our team." He noted revenue growth of 16%, Elastic cloud revenue growth of 23%, and a non-GAAP operating margin of 15%. Kulkarni emphasized the impact of sales strategy shifts, strong enterprise customer additions, and the growing influence of GenAI and consolidation as drivers for customer adoption.
  • Kulkarni described major customer wins, including an eight-figure expansion deal with a global financial institution and a multi-year seven-figure expansion with an international banking group, both leveraging Elastic solutions for observability and GenAI applications. He announced more than 2,000 Elastic Cloud customers using Elastic for GenAI use cases, with over 310 spending $100,000 or more annually.
  • Kulkarni also pointed to the general availability of the BBQ technology for vector compression and a new strategic collaboration with AWS, stating, "We announced a new five-year strategic collaboration agreement with AWS, which deepens our partnership through solution integrations, joint go-to-market, and marketing initiatives."
  • CFO Navam Welihinda remarked, "We finished the year strong and outperformed against the high end of both our revenue and profitability guidance. Our total revenue in the fourth quarter was $388 million, growing 16% on an as reported and constant currency basis." Welihinda noted subscription revenue of $362 million and Elastic Cloud revenue growth of 23%. He added, "Our current remaining performance obligations or CRPO in Q4...was approximately $1 billion and grew 18% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Welihinda provided guidance for fiscal year 2026, stating, "We expect total revenue in the range of $1.655 billion to $1.67 billion. Representing 12% year-over-year growth at the midpoint or 11% year-over-year constant currency growth at the midpoint." For Q1 fiscal 2026, revenue is expected to be between $396 million and $398 million, with non-GAAP diluted earnings per share in the range of $0.41 to $0.43. Non-GAAP operating margin is projected to be approximately 16% for the full year.
  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty, particularly extrapolating U.S. public sector pressures to the wider business as a conservative factor in guidance. Welihinda explained, "The lower end of our guidance range also assumes we see headwinds to consumption from Q2 to Q4, though we did not see them in Q4 of FY 2025."

Financial Results

  • Elastic reported total revenue of $388 million for Q4, with subscription revenue at $362 million and Elastic Cloud revenue growth of 23%. Gross margin for the quarter was 77% of revenue and operating margin was 15%.
  • Adjusted free cash flow margin for fiscal year 2025 ended at 19%. Welihinda stated, "We grew our adjusted free cash flow margin by approximately 600 basis points in fiscal year 2025."
  • The company ended FY 2025 with more than 1,510 customers spending over $100,000 and over 210 customers with more than $1 million in committed annual contract value.

Q&A

  • Pinjalim Bora, JP Morgan, asked about guidance conservatism. Welihinda responded, "I'm new to the business and I need to be prudent about the first guide...I've balanced the positive demand signals...with broadly macro uncertainty."
  • Bora also inquired about strategic partnerships. Kulkarni highlighted, "We are seeing real change in the market where we are being accepted as one of the most appropriate and favored vector databases out there."
  • Sanjit Singh, Morgan Stanley, questioned the state of RAG and cloud growth. Kulkarni stated, "RAG is being acknowledged as the solution that always makes sense...we feel very confident."
  • Singh also addressed sequential cloud revenue, with Welihinda attributing slower growth to the quarter having three fewer days and emerging seasonal patterns.
  • Tyler Radke, Citi, probed AI commitments and use cases. Kulkarni clarified, "25% of our million dollar customers are using us for some kind of AI workload." He noted emerging agentic AI use cases in sales, marketing, and legal sectors.
  • Howard Ma, Guggenheim Securities, sought clarity on cloud growth offsets. Kulkarni emphasized, "AI is helping us both in cloud and in self-managed," while Welihinda reinforced that both segments are expected to drive growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts consistently pressed for clarity on guidance conservatism, cloud growth drivers, and the impact of macro headwinds, reflecting a neutral to slightly negative tone regarding future visibility and growth sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident stance in prepared remarks and responded with caution and transparency during Q&A, using phrases like "we feel really good about the business overall" and "we have multiple paths to get to and exceed the guidance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current analyst sentiment focused more on risk, while management shifted from the prior quarter's upbeat tone to a more measured outlook, particularly regarding macro uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY 2026 projects 12% revenue growth at the midpoint, compared to 16% guidance for FY 2025 in the prior quarter, reflecting increased conservatism.
  • Cloud revenue growth in Q4 was 23%, down from 26% in Q3, attributed to fewer days in the quarter and emerging seasonal patterns.
  • The company added more than 45 net new customers with over $1 million in annual contract value compared to strong but unspecified growth in the previous quarter.
  • Management's tone shifted from highly optimistic in Q3 to more cautious and detail-oriented, explicitly citing macroeconomic and public sector risks.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from sales execution and product innovation in Q3 to guidance methodology, macro headwinds, and sustainability in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified U.S. public sector budget and personnel constraints as a source of sales cycle elongation and extrapolated similar potential impacts to broader guidance.
  • Macroeconomic uncertainty is explicitly cited as the main driver for conservative revenue guidance.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the underlying assumptions in guidance and the sustainability of cloud growth, reflecting concerns about visibility and external pressures.

Final Takeaway

Elastic's leadership underscored sustained momentum in GenAI, strategic partnerships, and customer expansion, while adopting a more cautious outlook for FY 2026 due to macroeconomic uncertainties and public sector headwinds. The company continues to prioritize innovation, cloud adoption, and enterprise expansion, with a balanced approach to growth and profitability as it enters the new fiscal year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cooper Companies outlines 10%–11.5% EPS growth for 2025 amid tighter revenue guidance and accelerated Myopia portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Cooper Companies, Inc. (COO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Al White, President and CEO, emphasized, “This was another solid quarter with consolidated organic revenue growth of 7%, led by double-digits growth in both our daily silicone hydrogel lenses at CooperVision and our office and surgical portfolio at Cooper Surgical.” He noted ongoing operational improvements and “OpEx leverage that drove double-digits non-GAAP earnings growth.”
  • The company reported consolidated revenues of $1.002 billion, with CooperVision contributing $670 million and CooperSurgical $333 million. White highlighted, “MyDay continued growing double-digits with particular strength in torics and multifocals, and our innovative Energy’s offering.” MyDay Energys was launched in Canada, and the upgraded Clarity One Day Sphere with WetLoc technology was released in Japan.
  • Myopia management portfolio revenue grew 19%, with MySight up 35%. White stated, “We just launched MyDay Energys with its innovative digital boost technology in Canada...and early feedback is extremely positive.” The company is focusing on reducing upfront fitting barriers for MySight, introducing promotions such as an initial one to three months free.
  • CooperSurgical’s revenue growth was driven by “success in our surgical medical devices, labor and delivery portfolio and PARAGARD.” Fertility revenues were $127 million, up 3%, but White cited “market softness,” especially in Asia Pac, due to declining cycles.
  • White announced, “We’re tightening and raising at the midpoint” of revenue guidance, but also reducing growth expectations for contact lenses and fertility due to lower market assumptions.
  • Brian Andrews, CFO, stated, “Consolidated gross margin was 68%, up from 67.3%, driven by continuing efficiency gains and mix.” He added, “One-line item worth highlighting this quarter is R&D, where expenses increased 21%,” reflecting higher investments in both segments.

Outlook

  • The company updated fiscal 2025 revenue guidance to $4.11 billion to $4.15 billion, “up roughly 5.5% to 6.5%, or up 5% to 6% organically.”
  • CooperVision’s revenue guidance is $2.76 billion to $2.79 billion, with organic growth of 6% to 7%. CooperSurgical’s guidance is $1.35 billion to $1.36 billion, up 3.5% to 4.5% organically.
  • Non-GAAP EPS guidance was raised to $4.05 to $4.11, “growth of roughly 10% to 11.5% year-over-year.”
  • Management expects “organic growth in Q4 to be stronger than Q3.” Lowered growth assumptions for both contact lenses (now 4% to 6% vs. prior 5% to 7%) and fertility were explicitly driven by recent market data.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenues were $1.002 billion, with gross margin at 68% and non-GAAP EPS at $0.96.
  • Operating expense increased 6%, but declined as a percent of revenue to 43.1%.
  • R&D expenses rose 21% due to new product development.
  • Operating income was up 11%, with operating margin at 24.9%.
  • Free cash flow was $18 million; CapEx was $78 million.
  • Net debt increased to $2.47 billion; leverage ratio improved to 1.9 times. The company repurchased 537,000 shares for $40.6 million.

Q&A

  • Jeff Johnson, Baird: Asked about contact lens market data and inventory. Al White responded, “Our FIT data remains strong. As a matter of fact, it's accelerating right now...we're starting to see a pickup in MyDay activity.”
  • Issie Kirby, Redburn Atlantic: Inquired about market growth assumptions and pricing. White replied, “Pricing is still sound...volume is still good. The mix shift...is driving a lot of the growth.” On fertility softness in Asia, he attributed it partly to cultural timing and tighter consumer spending.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked about consumer behavior and quarterly trends. White explained, “...if somebody is not buying a year's supply, but they're buying a three-month or six-month supply...all that stuff has an impact.” Andrews clarified that Q3 is expected below the guidance range, with Q4 above.
  • Jon Block, Stifel: Queried inventory trends and fertility. White said inventory pressure will persist this year. On fertility, “I think we probably swung that a little bit too far...I would be really surprised if Q3 and Q4 don't show strength.”
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citi: Questioned guidance conservatism. White admitted, “A portion of it to be fair is conservatism. I think that we needed to reset our numbers.”
  • Young Li, Jefferies: Asked about private label growth. White stated, “Private label biz for us at CooperVision growing a little bit faster than our branded business. No real surprise.”
  • Chris Pasquale, Nephron: Inquired about pricing. White said, “I think that pricing right now in the market is solid. I think future pricing is probably going to be a little bit higher than what it was pre-COVID.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed on the reasons for guidance reductions and sought clarity on inventory and market trends, reflecting a slightly negative to cautious tone.
  • Management acknowledged conservatism in guidance, market uncertainties, and inventory pressures, but maintained optimism on execution and product momentum. During prepared remarks, the tone was confident, but shifted to more cautious and practical during Q&A, with frequent references to market softness and inventory adjustments.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, both management and analysts were less bullish, with more focus on downside risks and a prudent stance on forecasts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance narrowed and growth expectations for contact lenses and fertility were reduced, whereas the previous quarter maintained higher targets.
  • Management’s tone shifted from optimistic to cautiously conservative, with explicit acknowledgment of inventory and macro challenges. Analysts in both quarters probed market share and growth sustainability, but Q2 saw increased focus on market headwinds and guidance prudence.
  • Myopia management and private label momentum accelerated, while channel inventory and consumer behavior emerged as larger themes.
  • Margin improvement and cost controls remained key, with additional R&D investments highlighted this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Softness in fertility, particularly Asia Pac, due to declining cycles and clinic spending.
  • Channel inventory reductions across the industry are expected to pressure revenues throughout the year.
  • Tariffs pose a $4 million headwind to costs in 2025, with potential 3% earnings impact in 2026 absent mitigation.
  • Currency headwinds remain, though mitigated from earlier projections.
  • Management is monitoring consumer behavior—shorter supply purchases and hesitancy on upfront costs for MySight.

Final Takeaway

Cooper Companies delivered another quarter of solid operational execution, highlighted by revenue growth in its core portfolios and raised earnings guidance. However, the company adopted a more cautious approach by tightening and lowering revenue expectations due to softening market growth in contact lenses and fertility, ongoing inventory pressures, and external headwinds such as tariffs and currency effects. Key product launches and aggressive moves in myopia management are expected to drive stronger growth in the fourth quarter, with management remaining confident in market share gains and the long-term outlook despite near-term conservatism.
